speaker: interviewer
text: --L-A-N-C-B-A-U-M. This is roll number four.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you told-- were telling me that you sent some things from Bergen-Belsen. And you had moved into a larger apartment.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And from that apartment, then where did you move?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't feeling well when I came to the States. I didn't like it. It was dirty. Brownsville was all dirty. The-- the papers were flying. And I was sick emotionally. And I used to go to doctors. And later, I was pregnant. And I gave birth to my other daughter. They are apart 6 and a 1/2 years-- Florence. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And my husband wanted to do something, so he went in with a-- with a landsman. He bought a grocery-- supermarket. And it didn't go well. So he gave him-- with the-- he gave him-- he told him he should take out money to eat from that, and we ate what we had money. And my husband didn't feel well, because he was sick on his back and everything. He went-- he used to go-- he went three times, one after the other, to Mount Clement, Michigan, for beds. And I-- my little girl was five-- the older one. And I had to go to the store to help out. One time, she was running. She was blowing a-- a feather. She almost got killed in the street. She was running after a feather. It wasn't so easy. It wasn't so easy. And we sold that store. We lost all the money. And he went to another business, another supermarket. Over there-- made a living, but not-- not so much. And my children went to school. I sent them to Hebrew school. And they went to public school. My daughter graduate high school when she wasn't even 16-- both children-- they skipped. And I didn't want to let them twice, because they wanted to give them their SPs. Junior high once is enough, because some kids who are very bad laid it up. And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --later, my-- my daughter got engaged. And she married Henry Heckel-- nice family. And she finished graduate school. She was teaching Long Island College. And she made her-- she had all her thesis, but didn't-- she had to do one more test, but she didn't want to go. She-- she switched to brokerage. And my younger one went to Harper College. And she met a fellow. The only thing was-- I was hoping-- Jewish-- a Jewish fellow. He looks like a Shaigitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, they're very happy. They both have two children. Joshua and Julie is Shirley's and Henry's children, and Melissa and Mark is Florence and Barry's children. When you look back upon your experiences, why do you think that you survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just luck. Just luck, that's all-- nothing else.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you often think about the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, always. Some people, they don't want to hear. They don't want to-- they didn't even go to see But when I went to see I say to myself, I went through more.

speaker: interviewer
text: The war is over almost 50 years now. Why did you decide to make this tape and to tell your story now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my daughter was bothering me a long time. She wanted to write a book about us. And she has everything on a paper. But I don't know what she's going to do. I don't know. And my grandchildren, from kids, when they were small, I told them my story-- not everything-- but when I-- summertime, they came to the country. I was in the Catskills. I had to pay them. I put on a-- I put on a corset.

speaker: interviewee
text: Bubbe, why did you put on a corset? I said, because of my back. Who did that to you? I said, the-- the German-- Hitler. And I told them. They asked me, what's that number? They know everything. They write about this-- stories-- in school-- that it comes out. And I want them to call me Bubbe and Zaydeh. That's what they call me. What is possibly your most painful memory from the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: I lost my-- my-- my family. My mother was maybe 50. I lost my whole family. It was very bad. Sometimes, when it comes [NON-ENGLISH]. I have yahrzeit. I can't stop crying-- comes everything back-- their names. If I donate something, I always honor for our parents. But now, we have to pray to have naches a little bit from the children-- from the grandchildren. They should go in the right way.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Do you have any message you would like to leave for the future generation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Future generation-- yes. First of all, there should be peace. Now, we-- we-- we should be proud. We have a homeland. And maybe, maybe, with God's help, there's going to be peace. And we shouldn't kill ourself. One shouldn't kill the other. Because by us, it wasn't that they killed-- it was a war. They just killed Jews. Most of them, they were Jews. Like, it-- it came, a-- a ship, here to-- and they sent them back. They went-- they went to concentration camps. They were killed. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] America didn't do much for us. Later, after the liberation, yes-- but nothing in the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I would like to thank you very much for telling your story today.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're very welcome. My pleasure. Should I introduce my husband?

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you please introduce this lovely man sitting next to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is my husband for 48 years-- going to be in January. And I love him very much, and he loves me. We're very proud of our children and grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Hyman Flancbaum is his name.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Would you like to tell us something about your wife-- something special?

speaker: interviewee
text: I met my wife in 1947. I was introduced by my first personal friend. She wanted I should marry her-- I mean, her daughter. And I was with her daughter in concentration camp. And I know her. I know her history. And I said to her, look. I know your daughter very, very well. She is not for me, and I am not for her. To her mother, I spoke open. I-- we know each other too good. And it wouldn't-- it will never be a good marriage. And I'm very sorry to say this to you. I know I'm going to hurt your feelings, but I have to say it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she said-- and then she said to me, you know what? I have a neighbor, a young lady, and she maybe will be good for you. What I ate yesterday night, I don't remember. But this, I remember. And I said, all right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she introduced me to that young lady. And to make this story short, it's going to be 48 years. I have a wonderful-- I have wonderful life. We-- we understand each other, and I'm very proud of her. We raised a beautiful family, and we are very happy. And I hope to continue to the end of my life with my Paula.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Would you please tell us where you got the tattoo? And read us the number.

speaker: interviewee
text: I got this in Auschwitz. The minute where we came in, when we stepped off the-- the trains, we got the tattoos. My number-- this was my name-- A-15589.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome. This picture-- I have two sisters here-- Esther Rifka and Schindel. And the rest are cousins. And this picture was made before the war, in 1937, on the bridge what the Germans were building. And later, they destroyed it. Nobody's alive. This was taken in Bialobrzegi, where I was living-- where we were living. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is me, Paula Flancbaum, and I was-- this is the night of the wedding, in 1948, January 28. And I have here with me two cousins. One is alive in Washington. The other one was killed. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This was taken in-- in Bergen-Belsen after the liberation. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is my cousin, Al Cooper, and his wife, Jean. And they're lovely people. And this was taken when we were there visiting them-- lunch-- in the United States-- OK-- I believe it's in Florida-- it was taken-- and not-- maybe a year ago. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This picture, this is my family-- my two daughters, Shirley and Florence, Henry, Barry, myself, and my husband, and cousins from my husband's side-- the Flancbaums-- and grandchildren, and my grandchildren, and my aunt's grandchildren, and a fellow, what-- he saved my husband's life in the woods. He is from Argentina. And this was made, probably 1991. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The place was-- my daughter Florence made a party for them. And this was in New-- in Larchmont, New York. This is my Aunt Paula and her husband Shloime [NON-ENGLISH]. And she is from my zaydeh's, my grandfather's, daughter and my mother's sister from the second wife. And I must say that my grandfather had 85 grandchildren and great-grandchildren. And she's alive, and her sister and brother died-- concentration camp. The place is in Boca Raton, probably-- the date, maybe '93.

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not yet. This is my brother, my sister-in-law, and they are married already 50-- 54 years. And this is their only son, Aaron, and the baby, Matthew. This was made last year, probably '90-- '94 in-- in Riverdale, New York. This is my husband, Hyman, and myself. And this was made maybe in here, in Boca Raton-- and year, probably '89. This is my lovely four grandchildren. Josh is 15. Julie, his sister, is 12. Melissa is 10, and Mark is 6. Melissa and Mark belong to Florence, and the other two, to Shirley. The place was at his bar mitzvah-- and temple-- Reform Temple, on 83rd Street in New York City, in '93.
